title: Is This The Best Butcher Shop In Texas?

chunks.title: Introduction to the Butcher Shop
chunks.summary: The host arrives at a highly praised butcher shop in Austin, Texas and expresses excitement about their offerings, including local seasonal fruits. He reflects on the local hunting-gathering food sources indigenous to the area, like apples and squash.
chunks.transcript: I came all the way to Austin, Texas because I had to see this place. I have heard this is the best butcher shop in Texas. And I'm excited because I love a good butcher shop. I went to an amazing butcher shop in Arizona. I'll put a link to that video in the description, but there's a little bit of my heart in Texas. Heart and Soil [music] is based here outside of Austin. Lineage is connected with Texas. I used to live here. They have amazing meat and you can even get raw dairy here. So, let's go into what is probably the best butcher shop in Texas and check this place out. First impressions, this place is gorgeous. Look at this. On the table, right as you walk in, they've got the fruit. So, not a ton of fruit because it's only local seasonal fruit. Here you've got the top of Texas Gala Apples, Deloreio Farms, pumpkin. If you lived in Texas right now as a hunter gatherer, as an indigenous human hundreds of years ago, basically all you'd be eating right now is pears, apples, and squash. chunks.title: Discussion on Vegetables and Greens
chunks.summary: The host shares his perspective on vegetables, emphasizing that while leafy greens contain nutrients, they may also have anti-nutrients. He explains his personal experiences with different greens, including chard and kale, and suggests that some might not tolerate them well.
chunks.transcript: so I definitely eat squash. Sometimes people ask about sweet potatoes. They're definitely food for humans. I don't feel great when I eat them, but that's just my physiology. I also don't feel great when I eat white potatoes. It makes my stomach feel like it's kind of gummed up. But if you can eat white potatoes, rock and roll, do it. Here's a question for you guys. What is your favorite apple? I'm kind of a fan of Fuji's, which is what we got here. These galas look pretty good. I think I'm just going to stick with the old standby Fuji. There's very few things more disappointing than a noncrunchy apple. Don't wish a non-crunchy apple on me, guys. Quick word about vegetables. Food for humans. Yes. Does everybody do great with them? No. In my experience, no. Leafy greens definitely have nutrients, but they have more anti-nutrients. For instance, you got chard here. Chard is pretty high in oxalates. If you guys ever get that charred feeling in the back of your throat where it kind of burns. That's kind of the defense chemicals in chard like oxalates like damaging your throat a little bit. I remember when I was a vegan, yes, I used to be a vegan. I made chard juice and the back of my throat just burned. So, look, if you can eat greens like this and you feel good, amazing. chunks.title: Local Sourcing and Quality of Meat
chunks.summary: In this segment, the host admires the butcher shop's commitment to sourcing from local farms, highlighting how knowing where your food comes from enhances its quality. He appreciates the transparency of the sourcing methods used at the shop.

chunks.title: Differences in Chicken Quality
chunks.summary: The host discusses the differences between common grocery store chicken and the Red Bro breed available at this butcher. He explains the issues with Cornish chickens and promotes the benefits of air-chilled, slow-growth breeds that are raised sustainably.
chunks.transcript: closer. And I really appreciate this. I mean, this butcher shop is going so far above and beyond to be so high quality. It's it's really impressive. Speaking of high quality and above and beyond, I was talking with the owner about their chicken. I got to teach you guys about this. So, they have this card here. Most chicken that you get in the grocery store is Cornish. And the problem with Cornish breeds of chicken apparently are that they grow so fast they must be harvested at 6 weeks or 7 weeks or they will die. They'll get so big they will break their own legs. It's crazy genetics that are optimized only for chicken production and have created this kind of mutant chicken. But all of the chicken here is red bro. So, Red Bros are 7 to 8 weeks, whereas the Cornish are 5 weeks. Most all Cornish cross breeds are water chilled with a chlorine disinfectant. What you really want when you're getting chicken is air chilled. So, when you go to the grocery store and you look at chicken and it says 5% retained water, that's water chilled with a chlorine disinfectant. That means you're eating chlorine in your chicken because 5% of that water chilling is retained in the chicken. Air chilling of the chicken prevents that completely. So, what they have here is Red Bro chicken, 8 weeks old, forage organic mix of regenerative ingredients. I think it's corn and soyf free, they said, but I got to confirm that. If not, they're moving toward that. And it's air chilled, so it's a completely different type of chicken than what you're getting in most grocery stores. And you can see here on the label, it says slow growth, harvested at 7 weeks, organic feed, moved daily in pasture sleds, fresh grass, bugs, and seeds. That's what you want. So, I generally don't eat chicken from the grocery store because it's just kind of meh. This looks like some of the best chicken I've ever seen. I challenge you guys. chunks.title: Final Thoughts on Quality Food Sources
chunks.summary: In the conclusion, the host encourages viewers to seek high-quality food sources and supports local farms and butchers. He emphasizes that improving food quality is essential for better human health, inviting viewers to prioritize their dietary choices.
chunks.transcript: Austin, you should drive from wherever you are in Texas to come check this place out. I think this is the standard. If there were more butcher shops like this, we would have access to such high quality food. It would really, I think, improve the health of humans. And I love what these guys are doing. there doing the hard work to create something that's very special and I'm just really excited to support them. So hopefully you guys found that valuable.
